coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive and selective determination of naringin using eggplant peel- derived nitrogen doped-carbon dots after extraction with Amberlite IRA-400: Evaluation the bitterness of grapefruit juice
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Alkahtani, Saad A.
Alqahtani, Yahya S.
Mohamed, Rasha M.K.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: This work presents a novel approach for the accurate determination of naringin (NAR) in grapefruit juice. In the proposed method, N-doped carbon dots were employed, which were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis using eggplant peel food waste as a green precursor. Detection of NAR relied on measuring the probe's emission response, which exhibited a decrease at 430 nm in the presence of NAR. The bitterness caused by NAR in grapefruit juice significantly impacts customer acceptability and can lead to economic losses if its levels exceed a certain threshold. It was found that the fluorescence quenching method showed excellent linearity in the concentration range of 0.0–2500.0 ng mL−1 (R2 = 0.9990), with a remarkable limit of detection(S/N = 3) of 16.37 ng mL−1. Furthermore, selective extraction of NAR from grapefruit juice samples was achieved using Amberlite IRA-400 ion exchange resin. The adsorption parameters were carefully selected, and adsorption isotherm models were evaluated to determine the adsorption mechanism. The efficacy of the developed method was demonstrated by its successful application in determining NAR levels in grapefruit juice at various stages of maturity. This study demonstrates the potential of the proposed method as a reliable and efficient tool for accurately determining NAR in grapefruit juice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Naringin
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Grapefruit
Amberlite IRA-400

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the fluorescence detection of NAR
Treatment of grapefruit juice samples with adsorbent resin
Optical properties and stability of EP@N-CDs
Determination of NAR in grapefruit juice samples
Quantification of NAR at various maturity stages

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/1).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (insets are size distribution histogram and HRTEM), (B) XRD, and (C) FTIR spectrum of EP@N-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared EP@N-CDs (λex = 320 nm, λem = 430 nm). Inset: EP@N-CDs imaged under natural and UV light (365 nm) light. (B) Fluorescence spectrum of EP@CD excited from 300 to 400 nm, at 10 nm intervals of wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the EP@N-CDs solution after addition of different concentrations of NAR (0.0 – 2500.0 ng mL−1). (B) linear relationship between F0/F against the concentrations of NAR.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NAR, fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of EP@N-CDs; (B) Fluorescence lifetime of EP@N-CDs solution before and after adding NAR; (C) Stern–Volmer plots (F0/F) for the fluorescence quenching of EP@N-CDs by NAR at different temperatures; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra before and after the interaction of EP@N-CDs with NAR.
Comparison between the proposed sensor and the other reported sensors for determination of NAR.
Isotherm model parameters of NAR onto Amberlite IRA-400.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: where Qm (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity of a single layer, and kL (L/mg), kF (L/g), and n are the respective constants associated with the adsorption isotherm models.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of NAR in grapefruit juice samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive and selective determination of naringin using eggplant peel- derived nitrogen doped-carbon dots after extraction with Amberlite IRA-400: Evaluation the bitterness of grapefruit juice

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Jouf University, P.O. Box 2014, Sakaka, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a novel approach for the accurate determination of naringin (NAR) in grapefruit juice. In the proposed method, N-doped carbon dots were employed, which were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis using eggplant peel food waste as a green precursor. Detection of NAR relied on measuring the probe's emission response, which exhibited a decrease at 430 nm in the presence of NAR. The bitterness caused by NAR in grapefruit juice significantly impacts customer acceptability and can lead to economic losses if its levels exceed a certain threshold. It was found that the fluorescence quenching method showed excellent linearity in the concentration range of 0.0–2500.0 ng mL−1 (R2 = 0.9990), with a remarkable limit of detection(S/N = 3) of 16.37 ng mL−1. Furthermore, selective extraction of NAR from grapefruit juice samples was achieved using Amberlite IRA-400 ion exchange resin. The adsorption parameters were carefully selected, and adsorption isotherm models were evaluated to determine the adsorption mechanism. The efficacy of the developed method was demonstrated by its successful application in determining NAR levels in grapefruit juice at various stages of maturity. This study demonstrates the potential of the proposed method as a reliable and efficient tool for accurately determining NAR in grapefruit juice.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bitterness poses a significant challenge to the citrus industry as it can greatly affect the quality and market value of citrus juices. Bitter juices are generally considered of lower value in the market, thus impacting the economic viability of citrus products. Although consumers may tolerate some bitterness in grapefruit products, excessive bitterness remains a major concern and a common source of dissatisfaction. Addressing this issue could lead to increased sales of grapefruit products by monitoring the excessively bitter products. The accumulation of two particular chemical compounds, limonin, which falls under the limonoid terpene group, and naringin (NAR), which is a flavonoid, is the primary cause of bitterness in grapefruit [1]. Typically, the highest concentrations of these bitter compounds are found in immature fruits, with levels decreasing as the fruits mature. Grapefruit juice has high levels of NAR (100.0 – 900.0 mg/L) [2], and considerable amounts of limonin (5.0 – 10.0 mg/L) [3]. NAR is the primary flavonoid bitter compound found in grapefruit, with a significantly higher concentration compared to other flavonoid bitter compounds. A common desirable quantity of NAR found in grapefruit juice is generally around 300 mg/L, while the minimum acceptable amount is set at 20 mg/L. This concentration provides a balanced and enjoyable bitter character to the juice. Juice with higher concentrations of NAR, ranging from 700 to 1100 mg/L, has also been found acceptable by consumers. However, at concentrations of 1500 to 1900 mg/L, the bitterness becomes too intense and is generally considered unpleasant. In order to increase consumer acceptance of juice, it is crucial to eliminate bitter components that can give an unpleasant taste. Previous researchers have reported various techniques to accomplish this goal, including chemical modifications, physical fractionation, mixing with non-bitter citrus juices and sugars, and enzymatic conversion [4–11].

A variety of methods for quantifying and detecting NAR concentrations in citrus juice have been reported. The majority of methods for identification and quantitation of NAR rely on HPLC-MS [12–15] and HPLC-DAD [16–20]. Electrochemical methods for detecting NAR include the use of voltammetric techniques [21–26]. UV–vis spectrophotometric methods are also available [27–30].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have gained increasing attention as optical sensors and probes due to their unique characteristics. With a size smaller than 10 nm and a narrow size distribution, CDs possess an aromatic carbonaceous core with multiple trap sites on their surface, which enable them to emit light [31]. CDs are highly preferred as fluorescent probes because of their selective and distinctive photoluminescence properties, as well as their excellent biocompatibility, good solubility, and stable dispersion in water [32]. In addition, luminescent carbon dots synthesized from multiple sources have been found to be effective probes for selectively and sensitively detecting metal ions, biomolecules, and photosensitizers. In this study, we have devised an eco-friendly and cost-effective method for producing CDs through hydrothermal synthesis, using peel waste from eggplant as environmentally-friendly precursors. Eggplant peel is the outermost layer of the eggplant fruit. The components of eggplant peel include fiber, antioxidants such as anthocyanins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, and vitamins C, K, and B6 [33]. Since eggplant peel also contains nitrogenous compounds like solanine, proteins, and nicotine, it is anticipated that the carbon dots prepared from the peel will be nitrogen-doped [34]. Doping carbon dots with nitrogen is crucial for a variety of reasons. Nitrogen doping enhances the optical properties of carbon dots, such as their fluorescence quantum yield and emission wavelength, making them highly suitable for bioimaging and biosensing applications. Moreover, nitrogen doping contributes to the chemical stability of carbon dots, rendering them more resistant to photobleaching and other environmental factors. Lastly, nitrogen-doped carbon dots exhibit exceptional biocompatibility, making them an ideal material for biomedical applications like drug delivery and bioimaging [35].

To the best of our knowledge, no previous fluorometric analysis has been carried out for the determination of NAR. The proposed sensor for NAR sensing utilizes fluorescence quenching of nitrogen doped carbon dots derived from eggplant peel (EP@N-CDs) when reacted with NAR. This sensor is designed for measuring the NAR content in grapefruit juice, which is a crucial step in assessing the bitterness and maturity level of the grapefruit.

The determination of NAR in grapefruit juice requires a selective extraction process, as NAR is present in complex matrices along with other compounds such as sugars, pigments, vitamins, and other flavonoids. Previous studies have reported the use of Amberlite IRA-400, ion exchange resin, for the selective adsorption of NAR [11,36]. Based on these findings, we chose Amberlite IRA-400 as the adsorbent for extracting NAR from grapefruit juice.

Fresh grapefruits (Citrus paradisi) were collected from Assiut university garden, Assiut, Egypt. Eggplants were procured from a local market in Assiut, Egypt. Naringin (99.0%) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). IRA-400Cl-, methanol, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA). Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, HCl, and quinine sulfate (99.0%), were supplied from El-Nasr Company for Intermediate Chemicals (Cairo, Egypt). All reagents were of analytical reagent grade.

UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on Shimadzu UV-1601 spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence Spectrometer with a xenon light source was used for all fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, the slit width was adjusted at 5 nm. The FT-IR spectra were measured on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) in the region 400–4000 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction pattern of EP@CDs was shown with Philips PW 1700 X-Ray diffractometer (Eindhoven, Netherlands). TEM images were recorded with the use of a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-1400, 200 kv, Japan).

To synthesize the carbon dots from eggplant peel (EP@N-CDs), the eggplant was first washed to remove any dirt or impurities. The peel was then removed and dried under sunlight for several days. The dried peel was processed into a fine powder by grinding. The powder was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated for 24 h at 200 °C. After heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the produced brown solution was purified by centrifugation at 8,000 rpm and using a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove insoluble small molecules. Then, the resultant solution was dialyzed using 3000 Da dialysis bags. The purified EP@N-CDs were then dried and stored at 4 °C for further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the fluorescence detection of NAR
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of determining NAR concentration, a 100 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 solution of EP@N-CDs was mixed with 100 μL of phosphate buffer solution (50.0 mM, pH 7.0), followed by the addition of 500 μL of NAR solution at different concentrations. The resulting mixture was then diluted to a final volume of 10.0 mL using methanol. After 1.0 min, the fluorescence was measured, with all emission spectra obtained under an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and an emission wavelength of 430 nm.

During the growing season (March to May), grapefruits at various maturity stages were collected from healthy trees. Ten grapefruits were selected based on their weight (150 ± 5 g), diameter (60–80 mm), and rounded shape. After washing, wiping, and peeling the fruits, a laboratory-scale juicer (Philips HR1832/00) was used to extract the juice. The juice was then filtered using Whatman filter paper no. 1 and centrifuged at 6,000 rpm for 15 min to obtain a clear solution. The freshly extracted clear supernatant juice was utilized for further selective extraction of NAR using Amberlite IRA-400.

Activation of the Amberlite IRA-400Cl- resin was achieved by being immersed in 4 N HCl for 24 h while stirring constantly. The resin was repeatedly washed with distilled water until it reached a neutral pH. Subsequent to this, the resin was treated with 4 N NaOH for 24 h, and again washed with distilled water until a neutral pH was attained. This process of activation and washing is essential to ensure that the resin is free from impurities and in its suitable state for the successful adsorption and desorption of NAR. By completing this sequence of steps, the Amberlite IRA-400Cl- resin can effectively and selectively extract NAR from grapefruit juice samples [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treatment of grapefruit juice samples with adsorbent resin
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction process of NAR from the juice involved several steps. Initially, 0.1 mL of juice was diluted in a 10-mL volumetric flask and 10.0 mg of Amberlite IRA-400Cl- resin was added. The mixture was subjected to constant stirring for 15 min at room temperature (25 °C). The Amberlite resin was removed from the juice using a combination of centrifugation and filtration through multiple layers of cloth. The NAR was desorbed using 0.5 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH, and after dilution in a 10.0 mL volumetric flask, the resulting solution was analyzed. To regenerate the adsorbent surface, it was washed three times with distilled water.

To conduct the adsorption isotherm experiment, 10.0 mg of Amberlite IRA-400Cl- was mixed with 10.0 mL of a methanolic solution of NAR, with the solution concentration range of 5.0 to 50.0 mg L-1. The mixture was mixed for 15 min at room temperature, after which the adsorbents were separated by centrifugation. The non-adsorbed NAR concentrations in the supernatant were then monitored using the proposed fluorometric method, and the NAR concentration was diluted if necessary. Using the obtained data, the equilibrium adsorption capacity (Q, mg/g) was estimated according to the following equation:
Q =
C o
-
C t
V
m
The initial concentration of NAR in the solution, denoted as C0 (mg mL−1), and the concentration of NAR in the supernatant after adsorption, denoted as Ct (mg mL−1), V (mL) represents the volume of the sample solution and m represents the mass of the Amberlite resin.

TEM and XRD techniques were utilized to examine the crystal structure, size, and morphology of EP@N-CDs synthesized. The TEM image of the as-prepared EP@CDs, as depicted in Fig. 1 A, showed well-dispersed spherical particles. The particle size distribution histograms revealed that the average diameter of EP@CDs was 2.5 ± 0.5 nm (inset). The high-resolution TEM reveals distinct lattice fringes of the EP@CDs, exhibiting a clear separation and spacing of 0.34 nm. This spacing is believed to correspond to the (0 0 2) plane of graphitic carbon. The XRD diffraction pattern, as shown in Fig. 1B, revealed a broad diffraction peak at approximately 22.2°, which corresponds to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of graphite. These findings suggest that the EP@CDs have a hybrid amorphous-graphitic nature. The FTIR spectrum presented in Fig. 1C reveals distinctive peaks that provide structural insights into the EP@CDs sample. The peak observed at 2906 cm−1 is related to C-H stretching vibrations, while the broad band centered at 3408 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (–OH) and amino (–NH) functional groups. The peaks observed at 1447 cm−1 and 1753 cm−1 associated with stretching vibrations of (C = C) and (C = O) groups, respectively. Furthermore, the peak at 1613 cm−1 is resulting from the bending vibration of the (OH, NH) groups. The presence of residual hydroxyl groups is indicated by the symmetric stretching vibrations of -C-OH and C-O-C, observed at 1006 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1, respectively. The XPS survey demonstrated characteristic signals of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s in the as-fabricated NCDs. These signals exhibited binding energies of 286 eV, 397 eV, and 529 eV, respectively, as depicted in Fig. S1A. Fig. S1B illustrates the C 1 s spectrum, displaying prominent peaks located at 282.4 eV, 283.2 eV, and 284.5 eV. These peaks can be attributed to C = C/C-C, C-N, and C = O/C = N, respectively. Fig. S1C displays the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s, featuring distinct peaks at 397.4 eV and 398.7 eV. These peaks can be assigned to pyridinic N and amidic/amino N, respectively. Fig. S1D showcases the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s, revealing characteristic peaks at 528.6 eV and 529.2 eV. These peaks can be attributed to C = O and C-O-C/C-OH, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of EP@N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of EP@N-CDs were examined using UV–vis absorption and emission spectra. The absorption spectrum displayed two peaks at 280 nm and 350 nm, which ascribed to π-π* and n-π* transitions of C = C and C = O on the surface of EP@N-CDs, respectively (Fig. 2 A). The emission spectrum exhibited a maximum wavelength of 430 nm and an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The solution appeared yellow when exposed to daylight but emitted bright blue luminescence when excited at 365 nm under a UV lamp (Fig. 2A). Excitation-dependent fluorescence was detected in the emission spectra of EP@N-CDs, which were measured at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 2B). The observed variation in the fluorescence was connected to the variation in size distribution and various surface functional groups of EP@CDs present in the solution. The quantum yield (Φ) of synthesized EP@CDs was determined to be 13.2 % by measuring the integrated fluorescence intensities of quinine sulfate as a reference, using the methodology described in previous publications [37].

To ensure the stability of the produced EP@N-CDs for various applications, several factors affecting their fluorescence properties were examined. The impact of pH alteration on EP@N-CDs' fluorescence intensity was investigated, revealing that the intensity remained almost constant over a broad pH range of 4 to 10, as depicted in Fig. S2A. Moreover, the impact of ionic strength was assessed, as shown in Fig. S2B, and it was noted that the fluorescence intensity did not change even when the NaCl concentration was raised from 0.1 mol/L to 1.0 mol/L. Furthermore, the influence of temperature (10-100℃) on the emission intensity of EP@N-CDs was evaluated, as shown in Fig. S2C, and it was observed that the fluorescence intensity starts to decrease above 40 °C. Finally, the effect of continuous UV irradiation was investigated every 10 min for 120 min (Fig. S2D). The results demonstrated that EP@N-CDs exhibit excellent stability to photobleaching. These findings suggest that EP@N-CDs can tolerate various pH values, temperatures, and ionic strengths, making them suitable for fluorescence sensing applications.

In order to enhance the emission intensity between EP@N-CDs and NAR, the experimental conditions were investigated including pH, incubation time, and volume of EP@N-CDs. The quenching effect of NAR on EP@N-CDs fluorescence was examined using phosphate buffer at different pH levels extending from 4.0 to 10.0, and it was found that the fluorescence intensity of NAR remained relatively constant across the studied pH range, with pH 7 producing slightly higher fluorescence (Fig. S3A). Subsequently, the suitable volume of EP@N-CDs was determined to be 100 μL after examining volumes ranging from 50.0 to 300.0 μL (Fig. S3B). Furthermore, evaluation of the reaction time revealed that the entire reaction process takes approximately one minute, as evidenced by the measured fluorescence emission at intervals from 0.5 to 5.0 min (Fig. S3C).

The species of eggplant used as the carbon dots source had a significant effect on the optical properties of the as-synthesized carbon dots. Three varieties of eggplant were evaluated Casper, Mileda F1, and Rondona F1. Rondona F1 peel yielded carbon dots with markedly higher quantum yield compared to the other two species. The measured quantum yield for the Rondona F1-derived carbon dots reached 13.2%, which was nearly 2-fold higher than either Casper or Mileda F1. The enhanced fluorescence suggests the carbon dots from Rondona F1 have fewer non-radiative pathways and defects that allow for more efficient radiative recombination. Given the superior quantum yield achieved with the Rondona F1 variety, it was selected as the excellent eggplant peel source for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots. The Rondona F1-based dots were used for all further fluorescence characterization and analytical applications.

The mechanism through which fluorescence quenching occurs during the reaction between EP@N-CDs and NAR was examined. The initial step involved measuring the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NAR and comparing it to the fluorescence spectrum of EP@N-CDs, as presented in Fig. 4 A. The results revealed that the absorption band of NAR completely overlapped with the excitation spectrum of EP@N-CDs. This spectral overlap indicated that the fluorescence of EP@N-CDs could be quenched by NAR through an inner filter effect. Fluorescence quenching of quantum dots typically occurs through either inner filter effect (IFE) or an energy transfer mechanism, such as Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). No overlap was observed between the absorption spectrum of NAR and the emission spectrum of EP@N-CDs, ruling out the possibility of an energy transfer quenching mechanism. To differentiate between IFE and FRET as the mechanism responsible for fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence lifetime was measured. This method was deemed the most accurate and practical approach to determine the mechanism of fluorescence quenching. Fig. 4B shows that the fluorescence lifetime of EP@N-CDs was measured in the presence and absence of NAR. The results indicated that there was a negligible change in fluorescence lifetime after interaction with 100.0 ng mL−1 of NAR. This finding suggests that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is IFE rather than FRET, as in the IFE process, the quencher does not chemically interact with the fluorophore, and it does not affect the fluorescence lifetime. To obtain a comprehensive understanding of this fluorescence quenching, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed, which can be described as, F0/F = 1 + Ksv[C], where F0 and F are the fluorescence signal in the absence and presence of NAR, respectively, and C and Ksv are the NAR concentration and the quenching constant, respectively. Fig. 4C shows the linear curves of Stern–Volmer plot (F0/F) versus the concentration of NAR. The Ksv values at 293 K, 313 K, and 333 K were determined to be 176 × 10-3 mL ng−1, 168 × 10-3 mL ng−1, and 153 × 10-3 mL ng−1, respectively. The Ksv values declined as the temperature increased, indicating that the complex's stability decreased with higher temperatures. Static quenching occurs when the quencher binds to the sensor in its ground state, which results in the formation of a non-fluorescent or less fluorescent complex compared to the fluorophore. However, as the temperature increases, the stability of the complex decreases, which in turn causes a decline in the constant associated with static quenching. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the EP@N-CDs was compared to that of the EP@CDs-NAR mixture. It was discovered that the absorption spectrum of the EP@CDs-NAR mixture differed in terms of both absorbance intensity and the position of the absorption peak. Specifically, the absorption peaks of NAR were red-shifted from 289 nm to 315 nm, and the absorption intensity increased (as shown in Fig. 4D), indicating the formation of a ground state complex. These findings support the hypothesis that both IFE and static quenching mechanisms were at play in the fluorescence quenching process.

The interaction between the Amberlite resin and NAR is facilitated by hydrogen bonding, which is the main driving force behind the adsorption process. NAR contains several hydroxyl groups, which are capable of forming hydrogen bonds with the nitrogen atom in the Amberlite resin [40]. Additionally, the phenolic group in NAR can ionize to produce H+ ions, which can form subsequent radicals that can be delocalized over NAR structure and easily form bonds with the styrene–divinylbenzene (SDVB) polymers in the resin. The SDVB polymers in the Amberlite resin demonstrate a strong attraction to conjugated double bonds, which are a common feature in flavonoid molecules like NAR [41].

Various factors such as sorbent amount, adsorption time, pH value, and desorption volume were evaluated. All experiments were conducted using spiked aqueous solutions with NAR concentration of 10.0 mg L−1 in triplicate. Fig. S4A illustrates that the extraction recovery gradually increased as the amount of amberlite IRA-400 increased from 1.0 to 15.0 mg, and then remained constant after 10.0 mg. This can be attributed to the increase in the amount of adsorbent, resulting in more adsorption sites. The highest extraction efficiency of NAR was observed when the amount of adsorbent was 10.0 mg. Therefore, the amount of amberlite IRA-400 was determined to be 10 mg. According to Fig. S4B, the extraction recovery reach equilibrium within 15.0 min. This rapid extraction time can be ascribed to the porous structure and high surface area of the adsorbent, which offer multiple adsorption sites for the target compound. To evaluate the suitable pH for the adsorption of NAR, various pH levels ranging from 2.0 to 10.0 were tested, and the results were shown in Fig. S4C. The data indicated that the highest extraction recoveries were observed at pH 5–7. It is important to note that the normal pH of grapefruit juice, after dilution, is around 5–6, which coincidentally falls within the pH range for the adsorption of NAR. Therefore, there is no need to adjust the pH of the grapefruit juice sample before applying it to the ion exchange resin. In order to recover NAR from the Amberlite IRA-400 resin after adsorption, a 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution was utilized in this study as an eluent. Sodium hydroxide is a strong base that can effectively disrupt the ion exchange bonds between the positively charged quaternary ammonium groups on the resin and the negatively charged NAR molecules. As demonstrated in Fig. S4D, the desorption efficiency of NAR increased significantly as the volume of sodium hydroxide increased from 0.1 mL to 0.5 mL. However, further increases in the volume of sodium hydroxide did not have a considerable influence on the desorption efficiency. Based on these results, a volume of 0.5 mL of sodium hydroxide was identified as a sufficient amount for NAR recovery.

The Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm curves were employed to analyze the correlation between the equilibrium concentration of the solution and the amount of NAR adsorbed on the surface of Amberlite IRA-400. The adsorption isotherms of NAR onto Amberlite IRA-400 were evaluated within the concentration range of 5.0–50.0 mg L−1 of NAR. The Langmuir isotherm characterizes monolayer adsorption behavior on the adsorbent surface, whereas the Freundlich isotherm describes multilayer adsorption on the heterogeneous surface of the adsorbent. Nonlinear regression was utilized to obtain the experimental data based on the isotherm models (Fig. S5) and summarized in Table 2 . The Langmuir model was found to be highly compatible with experimental results, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9986, which was higher than that of the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.9552). This suggests that monolayer adsorption is the common mechanism for adsorption, as described by the Langmuir isotherm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NAR in grapefruit juice samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was successfully applied to determine the concentration of NAR in grapefruit juice with high selectivity and minimal interference from similar compounds present in the juice. This was achieved through a combination of factors, including the use of Amberlite resin for selective extraction, the high concentration of NAR if compared to other related compounds, and a dilution step prior to extraction that reduced the impact of interfering compounds by dilution to 100-fold. Furthermore, the proposed method was found to be unaffected by other compounds that may be adsorbed such as limonin, which did not quench fluorescence of carbon dots and present in much smaller concentrations. The standard addition method was utilized to determine the concentration of NAR in grapefruit juice samples, as depicted in Fig. S6. After considering the dilution factor, NAR concentration in the grapefruit juice samples was ranged from 600 to 700 μg mL−1 (n = 3). To validate these findings, recovery tests were conducted by adding known concentrations of NAR to the juice samples. The results of the recovery tests, as shown in Table 3 , demonstrated that the proposed sensor had good accuracy for NAR detection in grapefruit juice samples, with excellent recovery values. The results indicate that the proposed method shows promise in accurately measuring the NAR content in grapefruit juice.

A selectivity study was performed to determine the ability of the developed sensor to detect NAR in grapefruit juice in the presence of different interferents frequently found in the juice such as glucose, fructose, limonin, naringenin, quercetin, ascorbic acid, hesperidin, neohesperidin and citric acid. The results of tolerance limits are illustrated in Table S1, at the concentration that produces a 5% relative error. These results demonstrate that the sensor is selective for detecting NAR. The tolerance limits were determined for each interferent, and it was found that the sensor could detect NAR at concentrations below the tolerance limits for these compounds. These results demonstrate that the developed sensor has a pronounced selectivity for NAR and can accurately detect this compound in grapefruit juice, even in the presence of interferents. The study revealed that the sensor exhibited good selectivity towards NAR, with no significant interference observed from other compounds commonly found in grapefruit juice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of NAR at various maturity stages
ce:section.ce:para: The designed sensor can measure the levels of NAR at various maturity stages of grapefruit. The fruit was collected at different maturity days during the growing season, which spanned from March to May. The sampling was conducted starting from day 100 up to day 250, with measurements of NAR content taken at 30-day intervals. The findings revealed that the NAR content declined with the maturation of the fruit, with the highest amounts being present during the early stages of fruit development. Fig. S7 clearly demonstrates this trend, which highlights the importance of monitoring the maturity stages of grapefruit to ensure optimal flavor and quality. The decrease in NAR content with fruit maturity can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, changes in the biosynthesis of NAR occur as the fruit matures, resulting in a slowdown or cessation of NAR production. This is due to a loss of flavonoid-producing ability in the cells of the flavedo, the outer layer of the fruit where NAR is produced [42]. Secondly, the dilution effect arises as the fruit volume increases during maturation, but the amount of NAR does not increase proportionately, leading to a decrease in NAR concentration [43]. Lastly, the conversion of NAR to other compounds, such as naringenin and neohesperidin, during fruit maturation reduces the overall bitterness of the juice, as these compounds are less bitter than NAR [44]. This study is important as it provides valuable information on the suitable harvesting period for grapefruit, based on the levels of NAR at different maturity stages.

In conclusion, this study introduces a novel and reliable fluorometric method for the accurate monitoring of naringin (NAR) in grapefruit juice using nitrogen doped CDs synthesized from eggplant peel. The detection of NAR was achieved by measuring fluorescence quenching of the sensor at 430 nm. Selective extraction of NAR was performed using Amberlite IRA-400 ion exchange resin. The adsorption of NAR onto Amberlite IRA-400 ion exchange resin followed a Langmuir adsorption isotherm model with monolayer adsorption being the dominant mechanism. This indicates that the adsorption process involves the formation of a single layer of NAR molecules on the resin surface. NAR levels were assessed in grapefruit juice at different maturity stages which provides valuable insights into the bitterness profile throughout the growing stages. This information can be utilized to determine the suitable harvesting time and aids in the production of high-quality grapefruit juice with desirable taste characteristics. The findings of this study demonstrate the reliability and efficiency of the proposed method for accurately determining NAR in grapefruit juice. This study contributes significantly to the development of analytical methodologies for characterizing and quantifying the bioactive compounds in food products, specifically detection and quantification of NAR in grapefruit juice.

Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Saad A. Alkahtani: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yahya S. Alqahtani: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Rasha M.K. Mohamed: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109358.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
